Wonderful suggestion. furl should absolutely support non-ASCII hosts via IDNA
encoding.

I'll add this shortly. Or, pull requests welcome.

That said, IDNA support is a bit delicate.

  1. Python's native idna implementation isn't up to spec: it supports IDNA2003
     but not the latest IDNA2008. See http://bugs.python.org/issue17305.

  2. The idna library (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/idna) supports the latest
     IDNA2008, but furl should require as few dependencies as possible. At best,
     the idna library becomes an optional dependency.

  3. As I've encountered independently, the idna library's `idna.encode()` barfs
     on perfectly valid URLs if the URL resembles an IDNA encoded URL. For
     example, `idna.encode()` barfs on the perfectly valid URL `u'xx--åäö.se'`:

     ```python
     >>> import idna
     >>> idna.encode(u'xx--åäö.se')
     Traceback (most recent call last):
       File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/idna/core.py", line 355, in encode
         result.append(alabel(label))
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/idna/core.py", line 276, in alabel
         check_label(label)
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/idna/core.py", line 239, in check_label
         check_hyphen_ok(label)
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/idna/core.py", line 134, in check_hyphen_ok
         raise IDNAError('Label has disallowed hyphens in 3rd and 4th position')
     idna.core.IDNAError: Label has disallowed hyphens in 3rd and 4th position
     ```

     If furl uses the idna library, it needs to account for this behavior.

I've since found I only get this error when I try to use the `URLType` as (part of) a primary key, which is what I was trying to do initially.

However whilst this worked with SQLite as a storage backend, MySQL for example complains that the underlying column type (`TEXT`) cannot be indexed without specifying how many characters to use so I've reworked my code so that I don't use the URL directly as a primary key and then the error disappeared and no monkeypatching is required.

Definitely. I will move furl into __furl/__ and add an appropriate __setup.py__ for PyPi.

Thanks for the suggestion.

I've been thinking about this pull request and issue #3 a lot over the last couple of days, reading about virtualenvwrapper hooks and looking for examples of how people use hooks (I've never used them myself).

Depending on which hook is being invoked, sometimes virtualenvwrapper sources the hook (e.g. postactivate) and sometimes it runs the hook in a child process (e.g. get_env_details). I'm curious about people's thoughts on running the hooks in the pew process or running them in a child process. 

Running the hooks within the pew process would allow the user to do things like setting environment variables or changing the working directory (although we'd need to think through the implications for the pew-inall command), but they wouldn't have access to the packages in the virtual environment or the virtual environment's version of python. Running the hooks in the pew process reduces the "environment variable problem" down to a straightforward user-provided script. Either based on the .inve.ini implementation, a Foreman-style .env implementation or simply altering os.environ.

Running the hooks as a child process (executed in the virtual environment) allows the user to work *in* the virtual environment and do things like starting a starting a development server or installing packages. In this approach setting environment variables would have to be handled as a special case.

Maybe the answer is to have some hooks run in the pew process and some run in the virtual environment?

I can prototype some options if it would be useful for discussion.

I also encountered this. It seems to be related to units that are dimensionless: I saw it with `"{:~}".format(ureg.cm/ureg.inch)` which simply gives an empty string. The long version works.

Digging around a bit: `pint.unit._Unit.__format()` does return an empty string if `self.dimensionless` is `True` (currently line 103). Units like "cm/in" or "count" currently have that set to `True`. There are two possibilities: 1) you want 1cm/in to be dimensionless, in which case the formatting code needs to be changed; 2) 1cm/in should not be dimensionless.

At the moment I'm using a build system that I rolled myself (github.com/benreynwar/pyvivado), but I thought it'd be nice to at least make it compatible with what you're doing.  I end up needing to generate code quite often, for example if I wanted to generate a tree with the depth specified as a parameter. 

The approach I've taken is for each module to subclass a Builder class which is responsible for specifying the necessary filenames for simple modules, and also for generating them for more complex modules (https://github.com/benreynwar/pyvivado/blob/master/builder.py).  This makes things nice and flexible, but I think your approach of writing a .core file will be something that people are more comfortable with.

Things start to get complicated when you start having the same generator with the same parameters used in different places in the design, or when one generator has a dependency on another generator where the parameters it passes to the second generator are some function of the ones it has received itself.  Or perhaps you have a generic module with a dependency on a generator where the parameters it passes depend on it's generic parameters.

I'm not at all sure on a good way to handle these kinds of situations.

Make sure you are running the latest released pysnmp.

Make sure you the clock wall on these machines behave normally. The exact time synchronization is not required with SNMP, though time should flow smoothly, no abrupt jumps here and there.

So you want to send `trap-type=10` with snmptrap command:

      ...
      -v 1 TRAP-PARAMETERS:
	 enterprise-oid agent trap-type specific-type uptime [OID TYPE VALUE]...

However generic trap type  10 does not exist according to [the RFC1157](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1157#page-27):

    generic-trap        -- generic trap type
                     INTEGER {
                         coldStart(0),
                         warmStart(1),
                         linkDown(2),
                         linkUp(3),
                         authenticationFailure(4),
                         egpNeighborLoss(5),
                         enterpriseSpecific(6)
                     },

That explains why pysnmp resets unknown trap types to `generic type`. So I do not see this as a bug in pysnmp. Do you?

Why do you need to generate SNMP TRAPs with supposedly non-compliant TRAP types?

For reference, [this is how you send custom specific-type](http://snmplabs.com/pysnmp/examples/hlapi/asyncore/sync/agent/ntforg/snmp-v1-trap-variants.html#custom-snmpv1-trap) with pysnmp.

Hey Tim,

As far as I can tell, this is working as expected. Basically, on your rebalance dates, the weights are indeed brought back to -1. 

Short selling is quite different from buying long, and that's why the weights are not always -1, as in the long case. Basically, when you short sell, you receive cash. The proceeds of the short sale are then passed up the tree to the parent strategy. You can see this in `result.backtests['short'].strategy.data`. This DataFrame contains the price index, the value (sum of all children values + cash), cash, and fees. 

Anyways, when the value of the short position changes, this can lead to situations where the weight exceeds -1 because a short sale implicitly creates leverage. Currently, `bt` does not enforce any margin requirements for simplicity's sake (as you would be subject to in 'real life'), but in theory this could be added (forcing you to cover your shorts if you don't have enough cash in your parent strategy). 

Hope this makes sense,
Phil

Hello Andreu,

Thank you for reporting a bug. The bug you're observing is due to a single set of values which were being used to convert viscosity in degrees Barbey to other viscosity units. The UnivariateSpline method requires the x-values to be continuously increasing or it could not perform an interpolation. For only Barbey out of 47 viscosity scales supported, the scale is an inverse and so the degrees Barbey decreased while viscosity increased. In previous versions of SciPy the error would only be raised if an interpolation was tried, not on creation, and I never tried a conversion with Barbey as I was developing the converter. I have added custom handling for it.

After fixing that, there is also the removal of SciPy's C2K and friends I had to fix. This project cannot make use of the new convert_temperature method introduced in SciPy 0.18 as it supports older versions of SciPy.  I have vendorized those functions in a new release of fluids.core, increasing the version of fluids that is required to use thermo to 0.1.68; its upgrade will be automatically installed when thermo is upgraded.

**Note this release includes some big changes (which should not be user-facing) in the identifiers.py file, to lazy-load portions of the chemical database for faster startup and reduced memory use. Please report any breaks from previous behavior this may have introduced**; I was not planning on a new release right now but I'd rather get a release out to fix this issue anyway.
The new thermo version is in PyPi, 0.1.38. Please close this bug if you can confirm thermo is working with SciPy 1.0.

Sincerely,
Caleb

I need `PatchedJediCompiledObject` because GObject Introspection that don't have overrides (eg. GtkSource) are basically class instances of `IntrospectionModule`, and `IntrospectionModule` is basically empty (`_names_dict_ensure_one_dict` calls `LazyNamesDict` with `self._cls()`, which would be `IntrospectionModule`, which is empty), not patching it there would mean Jedi would only be able to provide completions for GObject Introspection modules that have overrides.

Overrides in GObject Introspection are basically python files that wrap around the module to make some APIs nicer, they are not used in many libraries because usually the automatic bindings just do the right thing.

Regarding `auto_import_modules`, as I mentioned before (when you suggested to just always import Gtk), it's unfortunately not good enough, because Gtk is not the only library available via pygobject... there are many many others. In a common Gtk app you would likely use more than just Gtk itself, you could use GLib, gstreamer, GIO, GnomeBluetooth, GUdev... and many many others.

GObject Introspection is not just for Gtk, it's a generic system that allows you to get automatic language bindings for C libraries that use GObject, so the platform libraries are written in C and then via GObject Introspection you can use them from Python, JavaScript, Vala, and so on, without the developer having to generate bindings for each language manually.

Pid 0 on Linux does not exist.

Furthermore, if you send a kill with pid=0, you are sending the signal to the entire process group.

From kill(2):
> If pid equals 0, then sig is sent to every process in the process group of the calling process.

From kill(1):
>Specify the list of processes that kill should signal. Each pid can be one of five things:
>
>n
>where n is larger than 0. The process with pid n will be signaled.
>
>0
>
>All processes in the current process group are signaled.
>
>-1
>
>All processes with pid larger than 1 will be signaled.
>
>-n
>
>where n is larger than 1. All processes in process group n are signaled. When an argument of the form '->n' is given, and it is meant to denote a process group, either the signal must be specified first, or the >argument must be preceded by a '--' option, otherwise it will be taken as the signal to send.
>
> commandname

Just edit the message sending the new keyboard in `reply_markup`, you don't even have to delete the previous keyboard.

If you call [`editMessageText`](http://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#telepot.Bot.editMessageText), the keyboard of the message will be replaced by whatever new keyboard you pass to `reply_markup` parameter (including `None`, which will remove the keyboard). Of course, if you don't need to update the text of the message itself, calling [`editMessageReplyMarkup`](http://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#telepot.Bot.editMessageReplyMarkup) will also replace the keyboard with whatever you pass to `reply_markup`.

@tvoinarovskyi - I put the compatibility logic into KafkaConsumer and left the Coordinator classes to simply enforce constraints. KafkaConsumer will check the api_version after KafkaClient bootstrap, and if < 0.10.1 (max_poll_interval_ms not supported in JoinGroupRequest) then it will set max_poll_interval_ms = session_timeout_ms . In addition, if the user provides max_poll_interval_ms but not session_timeout_ms, we use the same value for session_timeout_ms. If the user provides neither, we use 30000/30000 defaults instead of the 10000/300000 used when max_poll_interval_ms is supported.

version: "2"

If you would like to try a raw sockets implementation I could include in the project that would be awesome!

Start by forking the project, duplicate the `udp.py` module as `eth.py`, rename *UDP* to *Ethernet* (like `UDPActor` to `EthernetActor`), change the socket type request in the director:

```
        # ask the dispatcher for a socket
        self.create_socket(socket.AF_PACKET, socket.SOCK_RAW)
```
Add to the `handle_read()` function after receiving the packet to split out the destination address, source address, length, and DSAP and SSAP fields.  Check to make sure that DSAP and SSAP are 0x82 for BACnet.  The rest of the content goes into the pduData field for a PDU going up the stack.

Add to the `handle_write()` function after getting the request to build an Ethernet frame with the same header fields (make sure you get the length right) and the rest of the packet is the pduData attribute.

In the BVLL service layer there is code for recognizing Annex-H vs Annex-J UDP packets, which you won't need, but there is code for recognizing when the source and destination addresses are local broadcast, so you'll need to translate those as well.

I'll leave this issue open for your comments and for me to add this in the future, otherwise you can close it and let me know what your fork is called.

This has been merged into a new branch `78-device-communication-control` and more comments and testing will be on the #78 issue:

* the `DeviceCommunicationControlRequestEnableDisable` enumeration had a
wrong enumeration value, it should have been `disableInitiation`

* moving the DCC state checking variable (combining the two you had into one)
from the `LocalDevice` object to the `StateMachineAccessPoint` where it is
used makes it easier to understand and the SMAP no longer needs the
`_localDevice` reference

* to check for the correct service, like allowing upstream Who-Is and downstream
I-Am, both the `apduType` and `apduService` values need to be checked

* the `timeDuration` is in minutes and the `install_task()` function has
a delta keyword parameter which uses the task manager to get the time.

* the `errorClass` and `errorCode` parameters are enumeration values so they
do not need to be instantiated as enumerated objects before passing
them to `Error()`

* errors can be raised and they will be turned into error PDU's sent to the
client

* by removing the requirement for the SMAP to have a `_localDevice` reference
I moved the `self.smap = StateMachineAccessPoint(...)` statement back
to where it was in the `BIPSimpleApplication()` initializer

* by moving the enable/disable state from the `LocalDeviceObject` to the SMAP
means that it no longer needs an `enable_communications()` function

* I made `enable_communications()` and `disable_communications()` to normal
methods rather than private ones so if an application needs to be notified
when these change they can be easily overridden

I added a sample application that generates DCC requests and I'll be using that for testing.

@BastienAr Having skimmed through this now, I think this restructuring is right along the behaviour I was looking for!   It's cleaner, it lets us test the JUnit bits independently, and it means we don't have to intermix logging with execution logic. 

One thing to change: you used the start_macro and end_macro callbacks within the run_testset method to mark its beginning and end, and start_test_case for individual tests: A Macro *is* a test (or benchmark, or parameterized testcase, eventually maybe a load test).   The start/end macro callbacks are for beginning and end of a test, not a group.  You'll want to create a start_testset & end_testset method in your JUnitCallbacks that handles this.  I will add the appropriate methods to the main Callbacks (and we can add to the execute_testset method). 

Here's how we handle passing config information in to pick/configure the callbacks bundle: we create the Callbacks object here:  https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/pull/171/files#diff-72237f18d508a05bb8c65dc41c4c0de5R189  - we can look at the JUnit config option and if present use that instead of the logger.  The Callbacks can be initialized with file path, for example and store it in the instance.   The logic to read configuration and pick/configure the callbacks *should* be isolated into a separate method call though. 

For the file read/write we'll probably need to add a start_output and end_output callback (the latter called before exit) to cap off the XML file.  The easiest way to do the latter (a bit hacky, but I don't want to block you while I rearchitect around it) is to [use a destructor](http://eli.thegreenplace.net/2009/06/12/safely-using-destructors-in-python) on the Callbacks instance.

One other smaller thing to do in the future: don't add extra whitespace changes. I'm not going to ask you change it on existing commits because I don't wish to be [That Nitpicky Open Source Reviewer](http://classicprogrammerpaintings.tumblr.com/post/131117128769/%C3%A9douard-manet-from-1867-to-1869-maximilian-sends).  But, extra whitespace makes it harder to skim the diff, and in cases like this where I'm already going to have to do some messy merge conflict resolution, it can make the problem harder. 

Stripping trailing whitespace & PEP8 autoformatting are easily done periodically on master branch with regexes and automated tools (I've done this in the past). 

I haven't looked closely  at the inner logic of the callbacks closely yet (I am assuming it's following the mappings in #43 more or less).

Hi @jkugler,

Right now the library doesn't support different fixtures files for each test. That is about to change though. I have two Issues that I created that deal with running fixtures on a per test basis (Issue #2 and Issue #6). You can read through them if you're interested, but the short version is that the first version of Flask-Fixtures used decorator functions instead of metaclasses to specify the class and/or method that you wanted to run fixtures on. This had problems with how the library interacted with custom setUp and tearDown methods, so I had to change the overall design of the library to use a combination of metaclasses and decorators instead. I've finished the first part of fixing these bugs, i.e., the switch to metaclasses, but I haven't yet finished the second part, i.e., making it possible to select a fixtures file for each individual test function. I'm working on the second part now though, and it should be finished very soon, so keep your eyes peeled and thanks for using the library!

Christopher

It looks like there are two problems, encoding and JSON format:

# Problems
## 1. encoding
`sqlitebiter` not properly opening for utf8 input files some formats (include JSON files).
This cause an encoding error at Windows environment.

## 2. JSON file format
Original JSON file (`test.json`) that you mentioned in the previous comment is not convertible as it is by `sqlitebiter`.
Convertible JSON formats for `sqlitebiter` are very limited for now (details are described in [here](http://pytablereader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/reference/format_specific_loader.html#json-loader-classes)).

# Solution
## 1. encoding
I've fix a dependency package that loading files.
Please try upgrade the `sqlitebiter` dependency packages with `pip install sqlitebiter --upgrade`.
You could convert utf8 files properly.

```
>type utf.json
{
"sites": [
{ "name":"闖憺ｸ滓蕗遞・ , "url":"m.runoob.com" },
{ "name":"google" , "url":"www.google.com" },
{ "name":"蠕ｮ蜊・ , "url":"www.weibo.com" }
]
}
>sqlitebiter file utf.json
[INFO] sqlitebiter file: convert 'utf.json' to 'sites' table

>sqlite3 out.sqlite
sqlite> .schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS 'sites' (name TEXT, url TEXT);
```

## 2. JSON file format
You could extract convertible JSON data by [jq](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/download/) command from the JSON, and convert it by `sqlitebiter`.
If you have a certain database schema that you would like to extract from the JSON file.

```python
import numpy as np

# Your own initial positions of shape (3,3)

# Initialize optimizer, this creates a position and velocity matrix of shape (3,3)
options = {'c1':0.5, 'c2':0.4,'w':0.9} 
optimizer = ps.single.GlobalBestPSO(n_particles=3, dimensions=3, options=options)

# Provide your own init_pos
optimizer.pos = my_own_init_pos.copy()

# Do optimization below

I solved the problem.

While in django 1.6:

1. `pip install python-social-auth`
2. remove `"social_auth"` from the `INSTALLED_APPS`.
2. add `"social.apps.django_app.default"` to the `INSTALLED_APPS`.
3. add  `SOUTH_MIGRATION_MODULES = { "default": "social_auth.migrations"}` to `settings.py`.
5. Create and empty migration to freeze the app: `python manage.py schemamigration --freeze default default freeze --empty`
5. Create a migration to add the `Code` model: `python manage.py schemamigration default --auto`
6. Apply both migrations: `python manage.py migrate default`

When that is done, you have django-social-auth's and python-social-auth's migrations in sync. Thus after you upgrade to django 1.7 or higher:

1. remove from `settings.py` the previously added `SOUTH_MIGRATION_MODULES` option.
2. remove `"south"` from the `INSTALLED_APPS` (you may also need to uninstall it)
2. apply python-social-auth migrations:
    1. `python manage.py migrate default 0001 --fake`
    2. `python manage.py migrate default`

django.contrib.contenttypes.generic will be removed in Django 1.9 and generate RemovedInDjango19Warning in 1.8.
In Django 1.8+ GenericInlineModelAdmin moved to django.contrib.contenttypes.admin and GenericRelation to django.contrib.contenttypes.fields

To avoid warnings and errors we need to try import them from new place first and fallback to django.contrib.contenttypes.generic on ImportError (for older versions of Django).

#394 have few bugs with that:
import from django.contrib.contenttypes.generic first (and we still get RemovedInDjango19Warning in 1.8)
And on ImportError we try to get both GenericInlineModelAdmin and GenericRelation from django.contrib.contenttypes.admin (but GenericRelation located in django.contrib.contenttypes.fields, so we will get another ImportError in 1.9)

That is an interesting question.

There is a "hack" in place that does something along these lines, the `explore` method. You can pass combinations of parameters you want the optimizer to probe, and it will do so when initializing.

    bo.explore({'x': [-1, 3], 'y': [-2, 2]})

will add the points (-1, -2) and (3, 2), to the list of initialization points (together with some other points drawn at random. Obviously this is not a formal approach to your question, and has limited scope, but in case you didn't know about it, might be of help.

But yes, I would love a feature where the user could pass arbitrary parameter subspaces as having a strong "gut feeling" prior, and let the model handle it. I believe a carefully constructed kernel could encode this information (since it dictates the general type of function the GP can model), however actually translating it to an user friendly UI might be hard.

Hi! In my case, I needed to perform queries on different strings, but with the same results structure. For example, passing "polygon_text=1" to include place geometries. I think it's equally valid to pass these parameters as part of the query itself, but the issue is that the current implementation sanitises `queryString`, which prevents passing a query string such as `"London&polygon_text=1"` to `query`.

Whether or not the parameters should be part of the object, or only passed when calling query is really up to you. I found it necessary to modify the library either way, and it just fit my use case better to let the instance hold that data, since the parameters never changed in between calls to `query`.

You are correct on your assessment for `PENDING`. On "queued tasks" I meant tasks sent by a client but not yet started by any worker. The thing is that `RECEIVED` will only show tasks on the worker's queue (that is, that have been effectively removed from the backend and "sent/received" by a worker), but not the ones that are still waiting on the backend. For these there is no other option than to check the backend itself, but it's actually quite simple: http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/monitoring.html#monitoring-redis-queues

OK *sigh* hah - I've not even worked with Django for 6 months, so took me 10 minutes or so to get this going (also tried booting it with Py2, didn't realise your repo was only Py3 compatible).

[If you re-read the docs you should be able to see what's wrong](http://django-rest-framework-serializer-extensions.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage-serializers/#writable-foreignkey-relationships). In your example, you've defined `country` as both a *field* and as an expandable field. You never want to define something as both.

You need to remove the *additional* `country` field reference to get this to work. The point is, the `expandable_fields` and `fields` should not overlap, a field should be one or the other. You'll also need to **manually** handle the additional `country_id_resolved` field which you're left with. The reason that this field is left in place is because Django has already done the hard work of validating that the foreign key actually exists, and if you wanted to perform additional validation on the instance (e.g. should "London" be assignable to "Belgium"), you could do it more efficiently by grabbing the resolved instance.

So, in the case of this repo, we need to remove the duplicate field references, as well as `.pop()` the resolved ID from our validated data before creating, et voila!

Thanks, hope that helps. If you think the docs could be clearer, please feel free to submit a patch suggestion.

-----

### Diff
```patch
diff --git a/core/serializers.py b/core/serializers.py
index a4eac71..458ac5e 100644
--- a/core/serializers.py
+++ b/core/serializers.py
@@ -18,7 +18,7 @@ class CitySerializer(SerializerExtensionsMixin, serializers.ModelSerializer):
 
     class Meta:
         model = City
-        fields = ('id', 'name', 'country', 'url')
+        fields = ('id', 'name', 'url')
 
         read_only = ('id', 'url',)
 
@@ -28,3 +28,7 @@ class CitySerializer(SerializerExtensionsMixin, serializers.ModelSerializer):
                 read_only=False,
             )
         )
+
+    def create(self, validated_data):
+        validated_data.pop('country_id_resolved')
+        return super().create(validated_data)
```

Yep, it'll choose a "best index" by default. You can give `Query` a `CouchDB` database object and it won't complain. Here's an example I ran against CouchDB 2.0:
```
from cloudant.client import CouchDB
from cloudant.query import Query

# setup admin party CouchDB client
client = CouchDB(None, None, admin_party=True, connect=True, url='http://localhost:5984')

db = client['animaldb']
query = Query(db, selector={'diet': 'omnivore'})

for doc in query()['docs']:
    print doc['_id']
```
Results:
```
aardvark
badger
lemur
snipe
```
By querying the `/_explain` endpoint you can see the primary `/_all_docs` index is being used to service this request.
```
$ curl -sg localhost:5984/animaldb/_explain -H content-type:application/json -d '{"selector":{"diet":"omnivore"}}' | jq .index.name
"_all_docs"
```
For production, we advise you create a specific index to service these requests in order to optimise query time. See our [query docs](https://console.ng.bluemix.net/docs/services/Cloudant/api/cloudant_query.html) for more details on this.

I believe that all that needs to be done here is to fix this line of code: https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/src/cloudant/_common_util.py#L245 with `if keys is not None:` and add a test to validate the fix.

@apeforest @sxjscience I think backpropagation will be different for a SoftmaxOutput final layer, because the derivative of the softmax with temperature will have an extra factor of 1/temperature. This leads to an extra factor of 1/temperature in all the d error/d weight terms. (Someone is welcome to double-check my math.) So it's not the same as uniformly scaling the data. Another way to think about this is that the derivatives are still with respect to the unscaled data. In other words, d f(x/T) /d x is what is used in backpropagation, not d f(x/T) /d (x/T), where f is the usual softmax function (temperature of 1). See also the comment [here](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/1579601/what-temperature-of-softmax-layer-should-i-use-during-neural-network-training) by isarandi on the first answer.

import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
import time

def argmax_nd():
    gpu_device = mx.cpu(0)
    t = time.time()
    for i in xrange(100):
        with mx.Context(gpu_device):
            nd_a = mx.nd.array(np.random.randint(10, size=(176, 176, 100)))
            nd_a -= mx.nd.min(nd_a, axis=(0, 1))
            nd_a /= (mx.nd.sum(nd_a, axis=(0, 1)) + 1e-8)
            b = 0.38 * nd_a + mx.nd.ones(nd_a.shape) * (1 - 0.38)
            c = b.reshape((-1, nd_a.shape[2]))
            d = mx.nd.argmax(c, axis=0)
            y = d / 176 - 176/2.
            x = d % 176 - 176/2.
            m = x.asnumpy()
            n = y.asnumpy()
    print 'argmax time: ', time.time() - t

def argmax_np():
    t = time.time()
    for i in xrange(100):
        np_a = np.random.randint(10, size=(176, 176, 100)).astype(float)
        np_a -= np.min(np_a, axis=(0, 1))
        np_a /= (np.sum(np_a, axis=(0, 1)) + 1e-8)
        b = 0.38 * np_a + np.ones(np_a.shape) * (1 - 0.38)
        c = b.reshape((-1, np_a.shape[2]))
        d = np.argmax(c, axis=0)
        y = d / 176 - 176/2.
        x = d % 176 - 176/2.
    print 'argmax time: ', time.time() - t


argmax_np()
argmax_nd()
```

@yajiedesign Thanks for the help. I managed to get build working with VS 2017 Update 6, CUDA 9.2 and cuDNN 7.1.4, OpenCV 3.4.1, and MKL 2018 Update 3. 

I noticed a few mistakes in the build instructions [provided here](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/install/windows_setup.html).

Here are more detailed instructions provided here in case it might help others.

```
1. Download VS 2017 Update 6 Community from [this site](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/productinfo/installing-an-earlier-release-of-vs2017).
2. Install with options configured for C++ desktop development
3. Download CUDA 9.2.88 and CUDA 9.2.88.1, install with visual studio integration support enabled.
4. Install GPU drivers after CUDA installation, due to an existing bug breaking the Nvidia Control Panel if CUDA is installed after
5. Download OpenCV opencv-3.4.1 for Windows and extract
6. Download and install Intel MKL 2018 Update 3 and install
7. Run the following:
    "setx OpenCV_DIR "OPENCVDIR\build\x64\vc15\include"
    "setx MKL_ROOT "C:\Program Files (x86)\IntelSWTools\compilers_and_libraries_2018\windows\mkl"
8. Add OPENCVDIR\build\x64\vc15\bin to path
9. Clone Mxnet and start the build:
    "git clone https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet.git --recursive"
    "cd incubator-mxnet"
    "mkdir build"
    "cmake -G "Visual Studio 15 Win64" .. -DUSE_CUDA=1 -DUSE_CUDNN=1 -DUSE_NVRTC=0 -DUSE_OPENCV=1 -DUSE_OPENMP=1 -DUSE_PROFILER=1 -DUSE_BLAS=mkl -DUSE_LAPACK=0 -DUSE_DIST_KVSTORE=0 -DCUDA_ARCH_NAME=6.1 -DUSE_MKLDNN=0 -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release"
    "msbuild mxnet.sln"
```

About 2:
I think we're both on the same page that providing configuration on a per task basis is a must. 
And I agree that having KubernetesExecutor specific columns in the `TaskInstance` table is bad too. 
How we solved this was repurposing the celery-specific `queue` parameter from a `varchar(N)` to a `TEXT/varchar(MAX)` field and using it to populate a json config object that can be used to configure your executor's `run_async` command on a per-task basis. For us, that means configuring the docker image and the resources to run the task with. 

I think in general this is an improvement for airflow since it removes a `CeleryExecutor` specific feature and replaces it with a general configuration field for all executors. 

This still is only *static* configuration though. If you want truly dynamic configuration you need another mechanism for shared config passing and validation. (We implemented this with row-level locking in Postgres and adding a new config table, but it is ugly). 

When I say static vs dynamic configuration I mean:
     static configuration => configuration that you know about when you are writing your dag. 
     dynamic configuration => configuration that you know about only when the dag is part-finished 

Dynamic configuration is useful if you want to do things like:
- Have a task that counts the number of rows in a hive table
- Based on that count, configure a downstream task to have more/less resources

About 4:
Makes sense. If you don't care about readability of IDs though you can just use a UUID and call it a day.

> If you're shooting for a 5 minute settle period, do you really need watchman to execute a trigger? ... I've found that it is often a good idea to think about the problem being solved than the technology being used and see what the simplest solution would be.

That is an excellent point and a good suggestion and I admit I didn't consider this simple approach. Thinking about it now though, I think it would require some extra logic / state to avoid either missing file notifications or providing duplicate notifications -- if the cron trigger period was exactly the same or larger than the `mtime` check, then its possible to miss notifications, and if its less, its possible to duplicate them (which admittedly is not a huge problem so far, since these are processed by a human). In addition, in some cases, the file modification times are copied to our server based on the source, so an `mtime` check would miss these as well. So I'm not sure this is a great approach for us overall.

We do have a secondary use case which we haven't implemented yet, but we will want to send these notifications to an event system, where we can then do other automated processing related to these files. Watchman (in its standard mode) seems like a good candidate for this type of processing. At that point, we may move the email notifications into a different backend process that reacts to the events on the bus instead, and does its own debouncing as needed.

So all-in-all, I think we're fine with the `empty_on_fresh_instance` (if it works), and without any additional changes.

The difference between status 204 and method HEAD (because we allow empty responses with `content-type: application/json` etc.) is that HEAD is defined to specify `content-type` of the data that would be sent for GET request at the same URL: https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec9.html#sec9.4
>  The metainformation contained in the HTTP headers in response to a HEAD request SHOULD be identical to the information sent in response to a GET request.

There is no such demand for responses with status 204 so it seems reasonable to expect `content-type` shouldn't be set to `application/json` when body is empty.

> I wasn't sure Poller's return would guarantee that an entire multipart message

Yup, zmq messages are atomic, so a socket won't register as readable (by any means) until at least one whole multipart message is available.

You can combine a poll with flushing:

```python
poller = zmq.Poller()
poller.register(sock, zmq.POLLIN)
while True:
    # wait for an event to process
    evts = dict(poller.poll())
    if sock in evts:
        while True:
            # process all available messages
            try:
                handle(sock.recv_multipart(zmq.NOBLOCK))
            except zmq.Again:
                break
```    

But that's only going to do anything if several messages are going to arrive during the interval of processing a single message. And it's only really helpful if you have other, lower-priority processing that you want to preempt that would also happen during the poll iteration. Processing one message per poll iteration ensures fair-queuing of multiple streams, which can prevent starvation.

So I would start with the simpler poll mechanism:

```python
while True:
    # wait for an event to process
    evts = dict(poller.poll())
    if sock in evts:
        # process one waiting message
        handle(sock.recv_multipart())
```    

and see how that performs.

> Perhaps this whole issue could be resolved by just adding a see-also to the documentation for recv() and recv_multipart() as an alternative to NOBLOCK.

That's a good idea. Would you like to make a PR adding to those docstrings?

Use `pip` to install the zip file:
```
$ pip install https://github.com/glassresistor/django-oauth2-provider/archive/stable.zip#django-oauth2-provider
```
or specify it directly in `requirements.txt`:
```
...
https://github.com/glassresistor/django-oauth2-provider/archive/stable.zip#django-oauth2-provider
...
```

Tell `pip` to install from @glassresistor's [repository](https://github.com/glassresistor/django-oauth2-provider.git):
```
$ pip install -e git+https://github.com/glassresistor/django-oauth2-provider.git#egg=django-oauth2-provider
```
or specify it directly in `requirements.txt`:
```
...
-e git+https://github.com/glassresistor/django-oauth2-provider.git#egg=django-oauth2-provider
...
```

And just a heads up: unfortunately `pip` does not save where it installed packages from, so running `pip freeze > requirements.txt` will not write the appropriate URL to the `requirements.txt` file. C'est la vie, I guess.

Yep, the unquote(urlparse(uri)) constructs result in /C:/Apps/... path instead of C:\Apps\.. which causes jedi to add another C:\ to the path as it interprets the leading slash as relative path.

I was able to fix the issue by using the following function from LSP package as replacement for unquote(urlparse(uri)) in workspace.py

def uri_to_filename(uri: str) -> str:
    if os.name == 'nt':
        path = url2pathname(uri.replace("file://", ""))
    else:
        path = url2pathname(uri).replace("file://", "")
    # debug(uri, path)
    return path

Thanks for the clarification. The package where this arose supports both Python 2 and 3, so for the Python 2 case a possible workaround is to subclass it:

```python
class Extension(setuptools.extension.Extension,object):
    pass
```

and pass an instance of this subclass to `cythonize`. This ensures that a new-style class propagates around.

For the Cython issue mentioned in the OP, this proved unnecessary, because `cythonize` is supposed to receive an iterable of `Extension` instances, so passing a singleton `list` avoids the issue. But I noted the workaround here, in case someone is looking for a solution, and cannot avoid this (clumsy) one.

Example.

After pip install(before relocatable) script starts with:

#!/tmp/virtualenv/bin/python
# coding: utf-8
from argparse import ArgumentParser
import os
import sys

import requests
...


And after --relocatable it changes to:

#!/usr/bin/env python2.7

import os; activate_this=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)), 'activate_this.py'); exec(compile(open(activate_this).read(), activate_this, 'exec'), dict(__file__=activate_this)); del os, activate_this

# coding: utf-8

python /tmp/virtualenv/bin/my_script

SyntaxError: Non-ASCII character '\xd0' in file /tmp/virtualenv/bin/my_script on line 37, but no encoding declared; see http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0263.html for details

I need to think about it. At first glance I personally think we should *always* use x, y as the order, except for indices. This is what is used in most other places. For example the WCS class takes (x, y). I do like the possibility of using some kind of class/namedtuple to make this more straightforward for users, but am just (at the moment) opposed to still returning two arrays and just swapping them.

Another initial reaction is that I think there are more current users than you might think, and if we silently change the meaning of x, y -> y, x that could cause some majorly wrong results (not just an inconvenience). Even at this stage, I would say that we should preserve backward compatibility unless there is no other option than to break it and there is a clear benefit (not just 'elegance' for us). I've heard users who were pretty annoyed that things in Astropy 0.2 no longer worked - it doesn't matter if we tell users it's experimental or not, the more we break, the more we annoy them, the less they are likely to recommend the package to others. We will only reach 1000s of users if the early adopters like it and recommend it! So let's break the API only if there is a compelling reason, and there's actually no way to maintain back-compatibility.

Still, following the discussion on Vidyo I had the impression that there were  basically two kind of people present. The first two of the following list.

- Developers as you call them. Those guys you envision here follow the bleeding edge anyway, run on the latest code, don't need any release notes and thus don't want to care about them. Usually, however, they _do not_ run a productive system.
- There is (to my experience) a second sort of developers that are a bit more relaxed concerning the blood, and don't follow the latest line of every commit or only parts of the project. They'd probably like distilled versions from modules they don't really care too much about (assume them working and less relevant) while they follow more closely the stuff relevant to them. They are more likely to _run_ a productive system  or are the second or third level support.
- Mere mortals (like me) are happy if they can weed through  a _distilled_ version of important changes _after the year or so_ when `git pull` happens. People like me don't like this release with every commit idea at all. I fear, I have to run systems in production. Keeping those running is the foremost concern. New features are not. In my world a release cycle of 2 years is _quite fast_ but probably do able. Everything faster is _unusable_. So, it might well be that I have to read _several_ release notes in sequence, mentally diff them to get the relevant part. This makes them a bit more important to me than the individual commit message as such.
- _Users_ of invenio would IMHO also like a release note. Much more, they would prefer stable packages with sensible configs in a decent Linux (server) distribution and thus don't care too much about it as it's mainly the maintainers stuff. Still one likes to get again a distilled list of important changes. E.g. I usually read the release notes of my _core tools_ in detail to check if some changes affect me or if there is some useful new stuff. Ie. in an ideal world I for myself would fire up `vim` to read the release notes after `apt-get install invenio` on my Debian stable server and then edit only config files, but I'd not write any line of python at all to get it running. However, I fear these people are not and will never be targeted by invenio.  (One _can_ have dreams though ;)

I think before discussing  fullstops at line ends, the usage of lists vs. prose or past/present tense formulations of more or less English text  one should be sure to know the target audience of the resulting document. I admit I don't care at all if it's past or present, if it's a bullet point list or prose. I am with @jirikuncar that it should probably be on the level of email. (Czech, however, would be a different issue due to _my_ limitations ;)

Additionally, I frankly admit, that some of the few lines of code I was able to contribute to invenio in the current scheme sometimes took me a few minutes to write while I had some tough and longish fight to get the commit right (and still failed as some of you know all too well). OTH I only did it once in a while so it was always basically starting from scratch.

Still I'd argue that one should have at least the mere mortals of the above list in mind and probably invest some work for them even if it delays some fancy new something.

The transport is on the client side so it shouldn't matter to the server which layer on the client the compression happens. I got the thing working by adding these to lines to https://github.com/getsentry/raven-aiohttp/blob/04b2768fc33dbd702cce4db02e66f9fc84100a2e/raven_aiohttp.py#L37
```python
    def async_send(self, data, headers, success_cb, failure_cb):
        data = zlib.decompress(data)
        del headers['Content-Encoding']
        @asyncio.coroutine
        def f():
```

There are two things leading to errors:
- When the Content-Encoding header is set, aiohttp will compress the data with the given algorithm. That's a deficiency in aiohttp, but in this case it leads to the data being compressed twice
- When compression in enabled, aiohttp sends the request with Transfer-Encoding: chunked. Our sentry installation didn't cope with it. Turning compression off in the client (as in the example above) leads to aiohttp sending the request with usual Content-Length headers etc and resulted in a successful request.

I'm doing a pull req against aiohttp that will hopefully resolve the issue.

Agreed, the pad factor and the xinterp have no meaning if you are providing a k-space grid which you have already decided is dense enough (dk) to portray the galaxy to large enough x and spans enough (maxk) to reach all the k's where there is power.   Some user is making the choices of stepk and maxk in the case you're describing?  You will have to trust that he/she made a wise choice of k grid.

xinterp gained its original meaning by the assumption that the "real" continuous galaxy image was defined to be the input x-sampled image interpolated between samples by this function.  If you want to assume instead now that the "real" image is defined by some interpolation between your k samples, then you don't need an xinterp at all.  You asked whether you'd want an xinterp to interpolate between some x grid values that you produced by an FFT of your given k grid.  I would say the purist's view would be that if you want values at other k's you should produce another FFT from a resampled k grid.

The kinterp I would keep with the same choice as before.  It is up to the use to insure that he/she has constructed an image with a dk that is small enough that good interpolation in k space will occur.  Note that this is what the pad factor was for before - hence the dk on which the k grid is drawn should be substantially finer than the bare minimum if you want the k interpolation to be accurate at part per thousand.

As Mike says, kmax is easy: it's always just the k value at which the FT goes to zero (for your chosen definition of zero ;) ).  The stepk could most logically be the spacing of the k grid that was provided.  But recall that it will actually be used to determine the stepk at which convolutions of this object with others will be drawn.  So if you think your grid is actually finer than is needed for drawing, you could specify a stepk that is larger than the actual k grid pitch.

## Expected behaviour

### Returned outputs should be consistent

- `month:AAAA` should never be returned, and should only be `AAAA-MM`.
- `AAAA` should always be returned for periods of 12 months starting in January, or for periods of one year starting in January.
- `year:AAAA-MM` should always be returned for periods of 12 months starting in other months, or for periods of one year starting in January.
- `AAAA-MM:12` should never be returned.

### Inputs

These changes should happen in `periods.period` parser.

- `month:AAAA` should be deprecated.
- `AAAA-MM:12` should be deprecated.

 (or removed if bumping to major)

## Documentation

See https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/issues/675#issuecomment-277238521.

```
import dill
import typing

def test_typing_basic():
    o = dill.copy(typing.Tuple)
    assert o == typing.Tuple
    t = typing.Tuple[typing.Callable, typing.Any]
    assert t == t.__origin__[t.__args__]
    ## >>> [k for k,v in vars(t).items() if v != vars(o)[k]]    
    ## ['__subclasshook__', '__args__', '__tree_hash__', '__origin__']
    l = typing.List[int]
    assert l == l.__origin__[l.__args__]
    u = typing.Union[int,str]
    assert u == u.__origin__[u.__args__]
    w = dill.copy(typing.GenericMeta)
    assert w == typing.GenericMeta
    assert isinstance(t, w)
    ### NOTE: the below are weird: isinstance raises a TypeError ###
    j = dill.copy(typing.Union)
    assert j == typing.Union
    assert repr(type(u)) == repr(j)
    # slightly less weird, but still weird
    a = dill.copy(typing.Any)
    assert a == typing.Any
    assert repr(type(a)) == repr(a)
    b = dill.copy(typing.Optional)
    assert b == typing.Optional
    assert repr(type(b)) == repr(b)
    h = dill.copy(typing.ClassVar)
    assert h == typing.ClassVar
    assert repr(type(h)) == repr(h)
    ### NOTE: more? https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html
    d = dill.copy(typing.NamedTuple)
    assert d == typing.NamedTuple
    assert type(d) == typing.NamedTupleMeta

def test_typing_annotations():
    def doit(x: int, y: typing.List[int]) -> typing.List[int]:
      return y + [x]

    _doit = dill.copy(doit)
    #assert sorted(doit.__annotations__.items()) == sorted(_doit.__annotations__.items())
    #assert _doit == doit

def test_typing_newtype():
    UserId = typing.NewType('UserId', int)
    _UserId = dill.copy(UserId)
    #assert _UserId.__qualname__ == UserId.__qualname__
    #assert _UserId == UserId

def test_typing_callable():
    q = typing.Callable[[int,str,typing.Any], typing.List[typing.Any]]
    assert q == q.__origin__[list(q.__args__[:-1]),q.__args__[-1]]
    c = typing.Callable[[int], None]
    assert c == c.__origin__[list(c.__args__[:-1]),c.__args__[-1]]
    p = typing.Callable[..., str]
    assert p == p.__origin__[p.__args__]
    w = dill.copy(typing.GenericMeta)
    assert w == typing.GenericMeta
    assert isinstance(q, w)

def test_typing_generic():
    T = typing.TypeVar('T')
    _T = dill.copy(T)
    #assert _T == T
    g = typing.Generic[T]
    assert g == g.__origin__[g.__args__]
    m = typing.Mapping[int,T]
    assert m == m.__origin__[m.__args__]
    i = typing.Iterable[T]
    assert i == i.__origin__[i.__args__]
    class User(object):
        pass
    U = typing.TypeVar('U', bound=User)
    _U = dill.copy(U)
    #assert _U == U
    e = typing.Type[U]
    assert e == e.__origin__[e.__args__]
    w = dill.copy(typing.GenericMeta)
    assert w == typing.GenericMeta
    assert isinstance(g, w)
    v = dill.copy(typing.TypeVar)
    assert v == typing.TypeVar
    assert isinstance(T, v)
    ### NOTE: needs work...
    s = dill.copy(typing.AnyStr)
    #assert s == typing.AnyStr
    assert isinstance(s, v)


if __name__ == '__main__':

I suspect that the problem is related to Scikit-Learn library version. You appear to be using SkLearn 0.19.0, but the SkLearn2PMML package was last tested & approved with SkLearn 0.18.2.

The above Java exception indicates that the internal structure of the `GradientBoostingRegressor` class has changed in SkLearn 0.19.0. The SkLearn2PMML package doesn't know about this change yet.

You could try two things at the moment:

- Just to be sure that the rest of your SkLearn pipeline works OK, temporarily replace `GradientBoostingRegressor` class with some other regressor type (eg. `LinearRegression` or `XGBRegressor`). The conversion should then succeed.

At some point Python moved to "even more stuff is in C" which appears to include the `_result` attribute. Perhaps it was always in C? But in any case, this attribute is no longer writable and is a "private" thing. So there's nothing else to do but remove the hack in txaio that makes `Future`s look a little bit more like `Deferred`.

There's already warnings in the documentation to not depend on Deferred-style "mutate the result" chaining. Now, you really can't because they'll behave differently in `asyncio` vs `twisted`.

We should also file an Autobahn ticket to audit for anywhere we might depend on Deferred-style chaining, but I do not believe we do.

I have run into the same problem today. I think it is very severe!  Basically if a wamp call is performed, and the outer task is cancelled, txaio raises an InvalidState exception which brings the connection down.

I cannot see why cancelling a task while it "awaits" a wamp call is an uncommon scenario. Below is an example which triggers the behavior. 

The suggestion above or a try-except block seem to work fine in my tests

```
import asyncio
from autobahn.asyncio.wamp import ApplicationSession, ApplicationRunner

class ServerComponent(ApplicationSession):
    async def onJoin(self, details):
        async def long_op():
            print("long_op")
            await asyncio.sleep(5)
        await self.register(long_op, "com.myapp.long_op")

class ClientComponent(ApplicationSession):
    async def _do(self):
        try:
            # this call will take a while
            res = await self.call("com.myapp.long_op")
        except asyncio.CancelledError as err:
            # so far so good, the task is cancelled correctly, but InvalidStateError occurs as soon as "long_op" returns
            print(repr(err))

    async def onJoin(self, details):
        f = asyncio.ensure_future(self._do())
        # cancel the task before long_op returns
        asyncio.get_event_loop().call_later(2, f.cancel)

    def onDisconnect(self):
        pass

if __name__ == "__main__":
    realm = "motesque"
    client_runner = ApplicationRunner("ws://127.0.0.1:9090/ws", realm)
    server_runner = ApplicationRunner("ws://127.0.0.1:9090/ws", realm)

    async def run():
        await server_runner.run(ServerComponent, start_loop=False)
        await client_runner.run(ClientComponent, start_loop=False)
        for i in range(0, 10):
            print("loop")
            await asyncio.sleep(1)

    asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(run())
```

In addition to the above, the web-socket base protocol uses `make_batched_timer` to reduce the number of timer objects we make when running with lots of connections. Currently there is one of these.

This would need to changed to be a per-loop batched-timer object. Twisted can use a single batched-timer (as now) and asyncio would need a per-loop implementation (so, push the batched-timer down to `autobahn.{asycnio,twisted}.websocket` from the generic class).

General comment: The docker best practice here is to specify all config as environment variables.  Then Beaker can pick up these parameters and display them appropriately in the UI.

For situations where there is a required config file that could be big, the docker best practice (https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/docker-user/FyCWLC38Ueg) is to use a wrapper script as your entry point that 

1) takes the environment variables that need runtime modification 
2) generates the required config file
3) launches the appropriate task.  

For reproducibility you could write the generated config file to the output path to save.

The Transfer API almost always returns JSON error messages, even for most 500 errors (if caused by internal python exception and not by Apache config). Other APIs may not be as uniform - I like the idea of guaranteeing that the error is JSONified if we get plaintext from the server, with maybe a size limit on the message length.

Not guaranteeing JSON in the case of uncaught python code errors in SDK/CLI seems reasonable to me - hiding them in a log file may just make it harder to get good github issues and support requests. The most important thing functionally for users is the nonzero exit code.

You are reading the documentation correctly, you are supposed to use question marks to mark that you want to use a parameter. From the error message, however, it looks like using a parameter marker (at least in the first location) is not supported by your database in this kind of statement. From the Exasol manual (I assume that is the database you are using), it is not clear which things may be parameters and which are hard-coded things.

Playing around with my local Exasol instance, I found that _all_ parameters in your particular command need to be plain identifiers/strings. So what you could do is something like
```
cursor.execute("create connection {} to {} user {} identified by {}".format(obj['system_name'], obj['connection_string'], obj['username'], obj['password']))
```

Unfortunately, that's just how file locking works on windows.  The running python process locks `vcruntime140.dll` against deletion.  Executing `rd /s /q` will delete everything it can; when it fails.  The whole operation isn't treated as a transaction, where if it fails, the changes are rolled back.  So issuing that command removes everything about `c:\temp\test` that makes it a conda environment, specifically the files in `c:\temp\test\conda-meta`.  Indeed at that point it no longer *is* a conda environment.  The definition conda uses to determine if a directory is a conda environment is whether there exists a `conda-meta\history` file or not.

On the other hand, you'll find that issuing `conda env remove -p c:\temp\test -y` *without* `rd /s /q c:\temp\test` won't remove the environment with the running python process, because the environment cannot be removed completely and cleanly.  It'll start to, but it does so within a transaction, so when conda gets to the `vcruntime140.dll` file and the kernel prevents a user space program from removing it, the transaction fails and conda rolls back all changes.

`is_reverse` is set when a read is aligned to the reference genome as the reverse complement of the read generated by the sequencer.  All alignments in SAM/BAM are relative to the forward strand of the reference genome, which means that the read must sometimes be strand-swapped. 
 i.e. to recover the original read bases, the sequence in the SAM/BAM file must be reverse complemented and the quality scores must be reversed.

The example that you show above (assuming R1 and R2 are properly paired in the FR orientation) occurs when the entire sequenced template fragment aligns to the reverse strand of the reference genome contig.  (You have the labels swapped above.)

> [Why does she go home]
> ...
>

1. You need to transform sentences into numeric vectors using e.g. [`CountVectorizer`](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.CountVectorizer.html) from scikit-learn.

I believe the whole confusion come from the flexible parameter `app_or_server`. This is a typical problem in Python because we can use any instance of anything we want anywhere.

If you use an Application instance, then `self.app` will point to the application. But if you use a BaseTestServer instance then `self.app` is that instance.

The error message users will see when trying to download a inconsistent project looks like this:
```
Error 404 on /files/<file_unique_id>/url
Reason:resource changes are still being processed by system
Suggestion:this is a temporary state that will eventually be resolved by the system; \
please retry request(env)
```

![eqs](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/15310535/23632739/20bf81a2-02d4-11e7-9155-07efaf7e805d.png)
The system of equations to solve looks like this. The derivatives of hamiltonian by angular momentum components are calculated from derivatives of hamiltonian by angles (theta, vaprhi -- the spherical angles describing the direction of angular momentum vector, they are passed to ```hamiltonian``` function). The thing is, that to construct the hamiltonian I need first to calculate the lagrangian (or the matrices that it is constructed from -- ```a```, ```A```, ```inertia tensor``` in code snippet above), here is the math form of the matrices ```a```, ```A```: 
![a_derivs](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/15310535/23632737/1e2d1558-02d4-11e7-845f-d0d2b98905b6.png)
There are derivatives of radius vector (of each particle) by internal coordinates (in the code snippet above there is the only one internal coordinate ```q```). For each particle I specify the functional form of a radius vector, so that in the ```hamiltonian``` these derivatives by internal coordinates could be taken. I think the radius vectors and derivatives of the radius vectors could be used in the numerical form, but it can get tricky for a system with multiple internal coordinates. And I don't see how it would provide speed up.

Regrettably, we do not have per user listeners. Users are 'fun' because we can either have profile wide changes or room wide changes to them (via m.room.member events which usually but not always are global changes, so context is everything!) . 

> I find that behaviour pretty simple and intuitive, but we could be more granular adding arguments to the two add_listener functions (like event_type='m.room.message' or event_family='state')

I like this. It might be the best way to do it because I think specific *functions* for each type is ridiculous. Potentially you might want to add a few wrappers for things like topic, but I'm not fussed. Event family is probably overkill, ``add_state_listener`` and ``add_listener`` would be quite fine. I also would add an option for exclusivity if people want it.

Allowing UTF-8 that way without further checks breaks the Matrix spec since you would allow non-ascii characters if the username had such characters.

So while it solve a problem, it creates another...

I ran into this as well and used another approach.

Example:
```
class King(Agent):
    self.wealth

class Bird(Agent):
   self.color
```

What I did was simply altering the uids.  E.g. all kings would have uids ['k0', 'k1', ...] and birds would have ['b0', 'b1', ...].  You get the idea.

The agent reporter would then look like:
`"wealth": lambda x: x.wealth if x.uid[:1] == 'k' else None`

I didn't really explore the above solution from Corvince.  But I just wanted to add mine here, which seems to work well for me and is stupidly simple.  

An alternative to changing uids would be to simply add a property to the class.  Something like Agent.type = 'king', and then verifying this when you collect data.

Me and @JobLeonard added some better error handling, and implemented the [context manager](https://jeffknupp.com/blog/2016/03/07/python-with-context-managers/) interface. This means that you can now connect to a Loom file like any other file:

```python
with loompy.connect("filename.loom") as ds:
    ....do something
# File closes automatically
```

Similarly:

```python
with loompy.create("filename.loom", m, row_attrs, col_attrs) as ds:
    ....do something
# File closes automatically
```

This is now the strongly recommended way to work with loom files, but is not always convenient. You can still use the old way, but then need to remember to close the file when done.

Plus, we now clean up if the file was created but writing the attributes failed, removing the broken file.

As a side effect of these fixes, it appears that loompy will now happily overwrite an existing file. I guess that's expected behaviour.

AtMost(a, b, c, 2) means a + b + c <= 2.
If you want to say a + b + c >= 2, then you can say so in the following way:

    a + b + c >= 2
iff
   -a - b - c <= -2
iff 
   (1 - a) + (1 - b) + (1 - c) <= -2 + 3
iff
   !a + !b + !c <= 1
iff
   AtMost(Not(a), Not(b), Not(c), 1)

In general, you can use a shorthand, along the lines of:

def AtLeast(vars, k):
      return AtMost([Not(v) for v in vars], len(vars) - k)

The project is definitely in a usable state, and is used to test a couple of professional projects today. 

Using the `pip install` method that [python-pact-testing-example](https://github.com/commoncode/python-pact-testing-example) outlines in the `README` should be enough to properly install pact-python. During that install process it will download a [separate package](https://github.com/pact-foundation/pact-ruby-standalone) to do the actual contract work.

There is a chance that something happened with the download of that package that caused the mock service to not get installed on your machine, but looking at the code today I think it should have blown up during install. We could certainly look at making this situation more friendly, by telling you which file is missing and that it could be an install issue.

If you are looking for an example, you might check out the [end to end](https://github.com/pact-foundation/pact-python/tree/master/e2e) tests of pact-python. These illustrate a lot of the major features of the project and [run during the build process](https://github.com/pact-foundation/pact-python/blob/master/Makefile#L32-L49).

The relatively long lifetimes for messages in the CoAP response cache is expected - RFC 7252 specifies a default `EXCHANGE_LIFETIME` value of 247 seconds.  The current OpenThread default limits the CoAP response cache to 10 messages.  Of course, the maximum number of message buffers held in the CoAP response cache depends on the size of the messages.

RFC 7252 allows for bypassing the response cache if the request is idempotent or the overhead/side-effect of performing the action on duplicate requests is preferred compared to caching the response.  DTLS has its own duplicate detection mechanism, so we could probably avoid caching responses for DTLS messages.  At the same time, there are a number of Thread-related CoAP interactions that we could bypass the response cache for.  That should alleviate much of the message buffer pressure.

I am not aware of a message buffer leak.  Let us know if you can confirm the issue and find the root cause.

Finally, 40 message buffers is probably on the smaller side for the Thread router/leader role.  At the same time, the number of message buffers needed depends a lot on application requirements and number of children supported.  This is the primary reason why the Thread Specification also allows a Thread router to advertise its buffering capabilities.

csvkit uses agate in its most recent version to support nearly all its functionality. agate only renders tables in a Markdown-compatible format (see https://github.com/wireservice/agate/issues/626), and there isn't much appetite to support a wide variety of output formats. We acknowledged this backwards-incompatible change across a major version (`0.9.1` to `1.0.0`) respecting semantic versioning. What is the issue with the change in format?

We know that type inference is slow, but we've gone over the default behavior in many issues over the years, and the decision has been that, while it occasionally causes issues, changing the default behavior to no type inference would cause many more issues. Just a quick example, users of `csvsort` expect dates to be sorted in date order, not in lexicographical order. Type inference has been the default for as long as I've worked on `csvkit`.

`csvkit` is not intended for cases where speed is critical. That said, we of course author and accept pull requests for optimizations, but we typically do not pursue performance at the expense of ease of use, code complexity, maintainability, extensibility, etc.

Anyway, for this issue, I'll leave it as adding `--version`. Please open a new issue if you'd like to pursue environment variables for controlling flags. You may be satisfied with creating aliases like `alias ncsvlook='csvlook --no-inference`.

@teto 
In your ``cmd2`` application, how are you sending your output to the screen?  

In general ``cmd2`` makes some important assumptions regarding how you generate output for users.  I'm thinking we may want to make it a little more clear in the documentation what these assumptions are.   The main assumption is that all output for the user is generated using one of the following three methods:
- ``self.poutout()`` - all normal output
    - This always gets sent to ``self.stdout`` which always gets redirected when appropriate
- ``self.perror()`` - all exceptional output (errors)
    - This always gets sent to ``sys.stderr`` which almost never gets redirected
- ``self.pfeedback()`` - for printing non-essential feedback (can be silenced)
    - Sometimes gets sent to ``self.stdout`` and other times to ``sys.stderr``

The ability to generate transcript tests from the history using **history -t** makes the above assumptions and it further assumes that only output written to ``self.stdout`` (via ``self.poutput()`` gets written to the transcript file.

As long as you are setting your custom prompt using ``self.prompt``, then it should be fine; transcript tests work with custom prompts.  Transcript testing goes a little beyond that even, it purposely strips off any ANSI escape codes from your custom prompt to so that it only compares text and doesn't compare color.

When you're doing more than just a few domain names, yes, I think so. Or maybe it's not *easier to execute* so much as it is *easier to read*. The `domains = ...` line is a flat list of domain names, so each element has only a single semantic context; the command line to create certificates, on the other hand, has potentially many arguments, each meaning very different things. Will this forever mire users in hopeless confusion between the two options? Any way out? Should we make `--privkey-path` have *either* meaning (even though they're quite different) depending on the context?

* Currently the CLI default `None` overrides the renewal config but it should not. Hence the option disappears on a subsequent renewal. I think I need to add a 

* There nonetheless needs to be a way to intentionally turn this off, which *should* override the renewal config and cause `reuse_key` to be removed from the renewal config. Should this be called `--no-privkey-path` or `--new-key` or `--new-privkey`? I assume we shouldn't try to get the parser to recognize the special string `None` as the Python value `None`, which would be ugly and confusing.

* It turns out that this functionality is still a bit confusing if you want to make a new key on first run but then reuse it afterward (for renewals). The reason is that the key-to-reuse doesn't exist yet on first run! So right now if you wanted to pin a key, you would either need to make it yourself with `openssl genrsa` and then keep your non-lineage copy around indefinitely, or you would need to make a new lineage and then immediately renew it with `--force-renew --privkey-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/privkey.pem` to give the renewal `privkey_path` something specific to refer to. Although some users who want to pin keys do have an existing copy of the key they want to pin, some people would prefer for Certbot to create the key on first run and then reuse it on renewal, and I'm not exactly sure what the UI for that common use case should look like.

The problem is the following. When `f` finishes executing, it returns a numpy array of zeros. That array is stored in the object store.

When you call `ray.get` to get the array, the array is still stored in the object store (in particular, it is not copied into the Python processes heap). Objects in the object store are immutable, and so the array values cannot be changed.

If you wish to change the array values, then you'll need to create a copy of the array locally and mutate that one. For example, the following should work.

```python
y = np.copy(x)
y[0] = 1
y += np.ones(100)
```

2. The notebook is loaded and some widgets are created. Some widget state came from the metadata, some came from live kernel execution
